skill.name: llm-application-dev-prompt-optimize
skill.description: Optimizes prompts for LLM applications using structured analysis, few-shot examples, evaluation plans, and model-specific guidance.
skill.summary: Turn rough LLM instructions into structured, testable, production-ready prompts.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported 62 potential issues, including command execution, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance patterns. Manual review found these are false positives from Markdown code fences, prompt examples, ordinary role wording, and checklist text. No executable scripts, network calls, filesystem operations, data exfiltration, or prompt injection attempts were found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Prompt Examples Flagged as Commands
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The static external_commands matches occur in Markdown code fences or resource references. They define prompt strings and examples, not executable shell or Ruby command execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are Markdown code fences, closing fences, headings, or resource references. No interpreter, script file, shell invocation, or user-controlled command path is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Keywords
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The weak cryptography detections match natural-language prompt engineering terms and example prose. No hashing, encryption, key handling, or cryptographic API usage exists at these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding context is prompt optimization documentation, not security-sensitive cryptographic code. The files are Markdown only and contain no implementation that could select a weak algorithm.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Reconnaissance Terms in Examples
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The reconnaissance detections are ordinary words inside prompt examples and validation checklists. They do not instruct host, network, or system discovery.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines appear in benign example text about edge cases, validation, prompt branching, and robustness. There is no command, endpoint, scanner, or discovery workflow.

content.value_statement: LLM application prompts often fail because requirements, examples, and output rules are unclear. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code produce structured prompts with testing guidance.
actual_capabilities: Assesses prompt clarity, structure, constraints, and output requirements.
Transforms short instructions into detailed prompts with roles, context, steps, and success criteria.
Adds few-shot examples, self-review checks, and error handling patterns.
Provides model-specific prompt structures for Claude, GPT-style models, and Gemini-style models.
Suggests evaluation plans, test cases, rollout methods, and monitoring metrics.
limitations: It provides prompt guidance, not automated benchmark execution.
Performance improvement estimates still require validation on real tasks.
It does not connect to model APIs or production telemetry by itself.
Domain accuracy depends on the quality of user-provided context and examples.

use_cases.title: Improve Support Assistant Prompts
use_cases.description: Convert broad support instructions into structured prompts with escalation rules, tone constraints, and response checks.

use_cases.title: Design RAG Answer Prompts
use_cases.description: Create prompts that cite retrieved sources, state missing evidence, and separate synthesis from unsupported assumptions.

use_cases.title: Prepare Prompt Experiments
use_cases.description: Define prompt variants, quality metrics, test cases, and rollout guidance before production release.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Prompt Rewrite
prompt_templates.prompt: Rewrite this prompt for clarity and reliability. Preserve the goal, add missing constraints, and define the expected output format: [paste prompt].

prompt_templates.title: Add Examples and Checks
prompt_templates.prompt: Optimize this prompt with three representative examples, edge case handling, and a short self-review checklist: [paste prompt and task context].

prompt_templates.title: Model-Specific Optimization
prompt_templates.prompt: Adapt this prompt for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code. Explain the structural differences and recommend the best version for production: [paste prompt].

prompt_templates.title: Production Evaluation Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a production prompt optimization report. Include failure modes, test cases, acceptance metrics, rollout steps, and monitoring guidance: [paste workflow].

output_examples.input: A vague prompt asks an assistant to answer customer questions about a product.
output_examples.output: The skill produces a structured support prompt with product context, escalation criteria, tone rules, response length limits, and verification steps.

output_examples.input: A RAG workflow needs answers grounded in retrieved documents.
output_examples.output: The skill returns a prompt that ranks source relevance, cites evidence, flags gaps, and avoids unsupported claims.

output_examples.input: A team wants to launch a new prompt in production.
output_examples.output: The skill provides a test plan with typical, edge, adversarial, and out-of-scope cases plus rollout and monitoring guidance.

best_practices: Provide the original prompt, target model, user goal, and known failure examples.
Validate optimized prompts with real task samples before production use.
Track quality, latency, cost, and safety metrics after deployment.
anti_patterns: Do not treat projected performance gains as measured results.
Do not expose private user data inside prompt examples.
Do not add hidden reasoning requirements when concise answers are enough.

faq.question: Does this skill run code or call external services?
faq.answer: No. The reviewed files are Markdown guidance and examples only.

faq.question: Can it optimize prompts for Claude?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes Claude-oriented structure and guidance for clear context, task boundaries, and output formatting.

faq.question: Can it help with Codex and Claude Code prompts?
faq.answer: Yes. It can shape coding prompts with requirements, constraints, examples, validation steps, and expected outputs.

faq.question: Does it guarantee better model performance?
faq.answer: No. It suggests stronger prompt structures, but real improvement must be measured with task-specific tests.

faq.question: Is it useful for RAG applications?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes patterns for source relevance, citation behavior, synthesis, coverage, and uncertainty handling.

faq.question: What input should I provide?
faq.answer: Provide the current prompt, target model, task context, desired output, examples, and known failure cases.
